food_name: Nitsuke Threadfin Whole Fish Set Meal
aliases: Nitsuke Threadfin Whole Fish Set Meal

english: Nitsuke Threadfin Whole Fish Set Meal

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for nitsuke-style simmered fish set meals. Mapped to the broader 'fish dish (food product)' class as the closest defensible FoodOn anchor. Exact nitsuke preparation is not represented in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish and fish products — cooked fish dishes
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for nitsuke (Japanese simmered fish). A00LC (cooked fish dishes) is used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. The set meal composite nature (fish + rice + sides) is not fully captured by this single code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for nitsuke simmered fish set meal. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular food preparation codes for Japanese cuisine dishes.

preparation_method: Braised in soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar
main_ingredients: Whole threadfin fish (Ikan Kurau / Polynemus spp.)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal serving of approximately 650 g, comprising a whole simmered threadfin fish, a bowl of steamed white rice, miso soup, and pickled vegetable sides.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fish, mixed species, cooked, dry heat (SR Legacy) and White Rice, cooked (SR Legacy); composite estimation for nitsuke set meal
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a nitsuke threadfin whole fish set meal. Nutrient values were estimated by compositing USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked fish (threadfin approximated by mixed species cooked fish, FDC ID 175168), cooked white rice (FDC ID 169756), miso paste (FDC ID 172443), and soy sauce (FDC ID 172237) in proportions consistent with a 650 g set meal (approximately 250 g fish, 250 g cooked rice, 100 g miso soup, 50 g pickled vegetables). The input record's per_100g values (energy 115.38 kcal, protein 10.0 g, fat 3.85 g, carbohydrate 10.0 g) are broadly consistent with this composite and have been retained with cross-reference to USDA data. Singapore HPB Foodo database was also consulted but does not contain a direct nitsuke threadfin set meal entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — White rice, boiled
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists for a nitsuke threadfin whole fish set meal as a composite dish. GI estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component: white rice, boiled, GI = 64 (medium) per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). The fish, miso, and vegetable components contribute negligible carbohydrates and do not materially alter the composite GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 65.0) / 100 = 41.6, where carbohydrate per 650 g serving = 10.0 g/100g × 6.5 = 65.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 276.9 mg/100g; approximately 1,800 mg per full 650 g set meal from soy sauce, miso paste, and pickled vegetables
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce in the nitsuke preparation and avoid or minimise the miso soup and pickled vegetable sides, as the full set meal delivers approximately 1,800 mg sodium — approaching the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg in a single meal; the threadfin fish itself provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids that support endothelial function.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice base with GI 64 and glycemic load of 41.6 per full serving; low dietary fibre at 0.62 g/100g limits glycaemic buffering
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request a brown rice substitution or reduce the white rice portion by half to lower the glycaemic load, while maximising the threadfin fish and miso components which provide high-quality protein and omega-3 fatty acids that support insulin sensitivity; mirin in the nitsuke sauce contributes additional sugar and should be requested in reduced quantity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat at 0.92 g/100g; threadfin fish provides omega-3 fatty acids; sodium is the primary cardiovascular concern at 276.9 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The nitsuke threadfin fish set meal is broadly favourable for cardiovascular health due to its low saturated fat content and the omega-3 fatty acid profile of threadfin fish (Ikan Kurau), which reduces triglycerides and supports cardiac rhythm; sodium management remains important and reduced-sodium soy sauce and miso should be requested to keep the full meal below 1,500 mg sodium for those with established cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nitsuke Threadfin Whole Fish Set Meal (煮付けイトヨリ定食; Malay: Set Ikan Kurau Masak Nitsuke) is a Japanese-style set meal (teishoku) served in Singapore, featuring a whole threadfin fish — known locally as Ikan Kurau (Polynemus spp.) — simmered in a classic nitsuke braising sauce of soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar, and ginger until the fish is tender and deeply flavoured. The nitsuke technique produces a glossy, sweet-savoury glaze that penetrates the fish flesh, yielding a moist, flaky texture with umami depth and a mild sweetness from the mirin and sugar. The set meal is typically served with a bowl of steamed white rice, a bowl of miso soup, and pickled vegetables (tsukemono). Nutritionally, the dish provides 115.4 kcal per 100 g, with 10.0 g protein, 3.85 g fat (predominantly unsaturated from the fish), and 10.0 g carbohydrate primarily from the rice component. Sodium is elevated at 276.9 mg/100g due to soy sauce and miso, making sodium management the primary clinical consideration. The threadfin fish is an excellent source of omega-3 fatty acids, supporting cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory health. The glycaemic index of the set meal is driven by the white rice component (GI 64, medium), with a glycaemic load of approximately 41.6 per full 650 g serving. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension and Type 2 Diabetes due to sodium and glycaemic load respectively, but low concern for cardiovascular disease given its favourable fatty acid profile. It is not suitable for halal diets due to sake and mirin, and contains soy and fish allergens.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for a nitsuke threadfin whole fish set meal. Nutrient values were estimated by compositing USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked fish, white rice, miso paste, and soy sauce in proportions consistent with the described 650 g set meal. The input record's per_100g values are broadly consistent with this composite and have been retained. GI is estimated from the white rice component using the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the composite estimation methodology is transparent, the dominant components are well-characterised in USDA data, and the input values are internally consistent.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite nitsuke set meal is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024; threadfin fish does contain omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids but a precise per_100g composite value cannot be defensibly assigned without a direct source entry. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish is not available in an approved source as a single entry; estimated potassium from fish and miso components would be approximately 200-250 mg/100g but this cannot be cited to an approved source for the composite. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite nitsuke set meal are not available as a single approved-source entry; USDA FoodData Central individual component data exists but composite weighting for this specific dish cannot be validated without a direct source. trans_fat_g is 0.02 g/100g (trace level) from the input record, retained as reported. The dish is not halal-certified due to the presence of sake and mirin (alcohol-containing ingredients). The ethnic_tags in the input ('Sushi', 'Tempura', 'Grilled Fish') are inaccurate for this dish and have been corrected in the output. Country of origin is Singapore (Japanese cuisine served in Singapore context). Source URL https://foodo.sg/ from the input record was reviewed but does not contain a direct nitsuke threadfin set meal entry and has not been used as a nutrient source. Multilingual local names added for Singapore context: chinese_simplified '煮付鲻鱼套餐', chinese_traditional '煮付鯔魚套餐', malay 'Set Ikan Kurau Masak Nitsuke', tamil 'நித்சுகே திரெட்ஃபின் மீன் தொகுப்பு உணவு', hindi 'नित्सुके थ्रेडफिन मछली सेट मील' — added to satisfy G6 and Q1/Q8 requirements for this dish served in Singapore. The dish is Japanese cuisine served in a Singapore restaurant context; both Japanese and Singaporean multilingual naming conventions have been applied.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1-2: Corrected food_name, added Japanese local name, replaced forbidden source with USDA FoodData Central, added GI, updated ontology match types, added health context conditions, populated aliases.

Iteration 3: Added mandatory Chinese (simplified '煮付鲻鱼套餐' and traditional '煮付鯔魚套餐'), Malay ('Set Ikan Kurau Masak Nitsuke'), Tamil ('நித்சுகே திரெட்ஃபின் மீன் தொகுப்பு உணவு'), and Hindi ('नित्सुके थ्रेडफिन मछली सेट मील') local names to local_names to satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance for Singapore serving context.
Iteration 3: Added all five new local-language name strings to flat aliases array and to aliases_by_language under their respective language keys to satisfy Q8 and aliases consistency rule.
Iteration 3: Updated notes to document multilingual name additions and rationale for applying Singapore multilingual convention to this Japanese dish served in Singapore.
